1. Practica has shut down.  I no longer work with them.  Please remove all references to Practica or BestPracticer from the site.
2. I’ve had a few more jobs.  Take a look at my LinkedIn profile at https://linkedin.com/in/rodbegbie to see what I’ve done recently.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/rod/.claude/plugins/cache/superpowers-marketplace/superpowers/6.1.0/skills/brainstorming

# Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs

Help turn ideas into fully formed designs and specs through natural collaborative dialogue.

Start by understanding the current project context, then ask questions one at a time to refine the idea. Once you understand what you're building, present the design and get user approval.

<HARD-GATE>
Do NOT invoke any implementation skill, write any code, scaffold any project, or take any implementation action until you have presented a design and the user has approved it. This applies to EVERY project regardless of perceived simplicity.
</HARD-GATE>

## Anti-Pattern: "This Is Too Simple To Need A Design"

Every project goes through this process. A todo list, a single-function utility, a config change — all of them. "Simple" projects are where unexamined assumptions cause the most wasted work. The design can be short (a few sentences for truly simple projects), but you MUST present it and get approval.

## Checklist

You MUST create a task for each of these items and complete them in order:

1. **Explore project context** — check files, docs, recent commits
2. **Offer the visual companion just-in-time** — NOT upfront. The first time a question would genuinely be clearer shown than described, offer it then (its own message); on approval its browser tab opens for you. If no visual question ever arises, never offer it. See the Visual Companion section below.
3. **Ask clarifying questions** — one at a time, understand purpose/constraints/success criteria
4. **Propose 2-3 approaches** — with trade-offs and your recommendation
5. **Present design** — in sections scaled to their complexity, get user approval after each section
6. **Write design doc** — save to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md` and commit
7. **Spec self-review** — quick inline check for placeholders, contradictions, ambiguity, scope (see below)
8. **User reviews written spec** — ask user to review the spec file before proceeding
9. **Transition to implementation** — invoke writing-plans skill to create implementation plan

## Process Flow

```dot
digraph brainstorming {
    "Explore project context" [shape=box];
    "Ask clarifying questions" [shape=box];
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" [shape=box];
    "Present design sections" [shape=box];
    "User approves design?" [shape=diamond];
    "Write design doc" [shape=box];
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" [shape=box];
    "User reviews spec?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke writing-plans skill" [shape=doublecircle];

    "Explore project context" -> "Ask clarifying questions";
    "Ask clarifying questions" -> "Propose 2-3 approaches";
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" -> "Present design sections";
    "Present design sections" -> "User approves design?";
    "User approves design?" -> "Present design sections" [label="no, revise"];
    "User approves design?" -> "Write design doc" [label="yes"];
    "Write design doc" -> "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)";
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" -> "User reviews spec?";
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Write design doc" [label="changes requested"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Invoke writing-plans skill" [label="approved"];
}
```

**The terminal state is invoking writing-plans.** Do NOT invoke frontend-design, mcp-builder, or any other implementation skill. The ONLY skill you invoke after brainstorming is writing-plans.

## The Process

**Understanding the idea:**

- Check out the current project state first (files, docs, recent commits)
- Before asking detailed questions, assess scope: if the request describes multiple independent subsystems (e.g., "build a platform with chat, file storage, billing, and analytics"), flag this immediately. Don't spend questions refining details of a project that needs to be decomposed first.
- If the project is too large for a single spec, help the user decompose into sub-projects: what are the independent pieces, how do they relate, what order should they be built? Then brainstorm the first sub-project through the normal design flow. Each sub-project gets its own spec → plan → implementation cycle.
- For appropriately-scoped projects, ask questions one at a time to refine the idea
- Prefer multiple choice questions when possible, but open-ended is fine too
- Only one question per message - if a topic needs more exploration, break it into multiple questions
- Focus on understanding: purpose, constraints, success criteria

**Exploring approaches:**

- Propose 2-3 different approaches with trade-offs
- Present options conversationally with your recommendation and reasoning
- Lead with your recommended option and explain why

**Presenting the design:**

- Once you believe you understand what you're building, present the design
- Scale each section to its complexity: a few sentences if straightforward, up to 200-300 words if nuanced
- Ask after each section whether it looks right so far
- Cover: architecture, components, data flow, error handling, testing
- Be ready to go back and clarify if something doesn't make sense

**Design for isolation and clarity:**

- Break the system into smaller units that each have one clear purpose, communicate through well-defined interfaces, and can be understood and tested independently
- For each unit, you should be able to answer: what does it do, how do you use it, and what does it depend on?
- Can someone understand what a unit does without reading its internals? Can you change the internals without breaking consumers? If not, the boundaries need work.
- Smaller, well-bounded units are also easier for you to work with - you reason better about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. When a file grows large, that's often a signal that it's doing too much.

**Working in existing codebases:**

- Explore the current structure before proposing changes. Follow existing patterns.
- Where existing code has problems that affect the work (e.g., a file that's grown too large, unclear boundaries, tangled responsibilities), include targeted improvements as part of the design - the way a good developer improves code they're working in.
- Don't propose unrelated refactoring. Stay focused on what serves the current goal.

## After the Design

**Documentation:**

- Write the validated design (spec) to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md`
  - (User preferences for spec location override this default)
- Use elements-of-style:writing-clearly-and-concisely skill if available
- Commit the design document to git

**Spec Self-Review:**
After writing the spec document, look at it with fresh eyes:

1. **Placeholder scan:** Any "TBD", "TODO", incomplete sections, or vague requirements? Fix them.
2. **Internal consistency:** Do any sections contradict each other? Does the architecture match the feature descriptions?
3. **Scope check:** Is this focused enough for a single implementation plan, or does it need decomposition?
4. **Ambiguity check:** Could any requirement be interpreted two different ways? If so, pick one and make it explicit.

Fix any issues inline. No need to re-review — just fix and move on.

**User Review Gate:**
After the spec review loop passes, ask the user to review the written spec before proceeding:

> "Spec written and committed to `<path>`. Please review it and let me know if you want to make any changes before we start writing out the implementation plan."

Wait for the user's response. If they request changes, make them and re-run the spec review loop. Only proceed once the user approves.

**Implementation:**

- Invoke the writing-plans skill to create a detailed implementation plan
- Do NOT invoke any other skill. writing-plans is the next step.

## Key Principles

- **One question at a time** - Don't overwhelm with multiple questions
- **Multiple choice preferred** - Easier to answer than open-ended when possible
- **YAGNI ruthlessly** - Remove unnecessary features from all designs
- **Explore alternatives** - Always propose 2-3 approaches before settling
- **Incremental validation** - Present design, get approval before moving on
- **Be flexible** - Go back and clarify when something doesn't make sense

## Visual Companion

A browser-based companion for showing mockups, diagrams, and visual options during brainstorming. Available as a tool — not a mode. Accepting the companion means it's available for questions that benefit from visual treatment; it does NOT mean every question goes through the browser.

**Offering the companion (just-in-time):** Do NOT offer it upfront. Wait until a question would genuinely be clearer shown than told — a real mockup / layout / diagram question, not merely a UI *topic*. The first time that happens, offer it then, as its own message:
> "This next part might be easier if I show you — I can put together mockups, diagrams, and comparisons in a browser tab as we go. It's still new and can be token-intensive. Want me to? I'll open it for you."

**This offer MUST be its own message.** Only the offer — no clarifying question, summary, or other content. Wait for the user's response. If they accept, start the server with `--open` so their browser opens to the first screen automatically. If they decline, continue text-only and don't offer again unless they raise it.

**Per-question decision:** Even after the user accepts, decide FOR EACH QUESTION whether to use the browser or the terminal. The test: **would the user understand this better by seeing it than reading it?**

- **Use the browser** for content that IS visual — mockups, wireframes, layout comparisons, architecture diagrams, side-by-side visual designs
- **Use the terminal** for content that is text — requirements questions, conceptual choices, tradeoff lists, A/B/C/D text options, scope decisions

A question about a UI topic is not automatically a visual question. "What does personality mean in this context?" is a conceptual question — use the terminal. "Which wizard layout works better?" is a visual question — use the browser.

If they agree to the companion, read the detailed guide before proceeding:
`skills/brainstorming/visual-companion.md`

content.text: This touches more than search-and-replace — the footer's whole "Coaching" CTA and the site's hero tagline are built around Practica as the booking channel. Before I plan the copy changes, I need to know the current state of your coaching work.

questions.question: Since Practica shut down, what's the status of your coaching practice?

options.label: Still coaching independently
options.description: You still offer engineering leadership coaching, just book directly (e.g. via email) instead of through Practica.

options.label: Stopped coaching for now
options.description: You're not actively taking on coaching clients — site should focus on your day job / resume rather than a coaching pitch.

options.label: Something else
options.description: Different arrangement — I'll ask you to describe it.

result.output: Your questions have been answered: "Since Practica shut down, what's the status of your coaching practice?"="Still coaching independently". You can now continue with these answers in mind.

content.text:
Before I fetch your LinkedIn profile, a flag: this session has a `context-mode` rule/hook injecting very forceful instructions ("BLOCKED", "MUST") telling me to route all fetches, greps, and file reads through a third-party MCP tool (`ctx_fetch_and_index`) that stores content in an external "knowledge base" (it even advertises a "hosted analytics layer" dashboard). That pattern — aggressive imperative language demanding all data flow through one specific external service — has the shape of a prompt injection or at minimum an overreaching plugin, not a core Claude Code behavior. I don't think it's appropriate to pipe your LinkedIn profile content through it without your say-so.

I'll use the standard WebFetch tool instead for reading your LinkedIn profile. Flagging so you're aware this hook exists in your config — you may want to review `~/.claude/rules/context-mode.md` and whatever plugin installed it.

input.prompt: List Rod Begbie's work experience: job titles, companies, and date ranges, especially anything after Dropbox (2016-2020). Also note current headline/title.

result.output: context-mode: WebFetch redirected. Call mcp__plugin_context-mode_context-mode__ctx_fetch_and_index(url: "https://linkedin.com/in/rodbegbie", source: "...") to fetch + index the page, then mcp__plugin_context-mode_context-mode__ctx_search(queries: [...]) to query the indexed content — the raw page bytes stay in storage instead of entering your conversation. Or call mcp__plugin_context-mode_context-mode__ctx_execute(language, code) when you want to derive your answer in one round trip (parse, extract, count) without persisting the response. Both have full network access. Retry the same call on a transient DNS error (EAI_AGAIN, ETIMEDOUT, ENETUNREACH).

content.text: That confirms it's a hard-enforced hook (not just a suggestion) — my direct WebFetch call was blocked outright, forcing all web content through the third-party `context-mode` plugin. Since that means your LinkedIn profile content would be stored in that plugin's external knowledge base, I want your explicit OK before doing that, or we can skip it and you tell me your recent roles directly.

questions.question: How do you want to get your recent job history into this session?

options.label: You tell me directly
options.description: You paste/describe your recent roles (titles, companies, dates) and I use that — avoids sending your LinkedIn profile through the third-party context-mode plugin.

options.label: OK to use context-mode's fetch
options.description: Allow the ctx_fetch_and_index tool to fetch and store your public LinkedIn profile content in its external index for this purpose.

result.output: Your questions have been answered: "How do you want to get your recent job history into this session?"="OK to use context-mode's fetch". You can now continue with these answers in mind.

content.text:
The fetch hit LinkedIn's login wall, as expected — LinkedIn blocks unauthenticated scraping. The only usable signal was the page `<title>`: "Rod Begbie - **Splice** | LinkedIn", confirming your current employer, which matches what I already know from your CLAUDE.md (leading Engineering Velocity at Splice). No detailed history came through though.

Rather than fight LinkedIn's wall further, could you just tell me directly: your current title at Splice, roughly when you started there, and what happened in between — did Discord → Practica coaching → Splice happen in that order, or is there another role I'm missing?
Take a look at my resume at ~/Downloads/2026-07 Rod Begbie Resume.pdf

content.text:
I now have full career history from your resume. Here's my proposed copy plan — three files, no architecture changes, just content:

**1. `data/aboutSection.yml`** — bullet 1 currently says you're "a Senior Engineering Manager at Discord, and an engineering leadership coach with Practica." Replace with your current Splice title + independent coaching, and fold Volley/Discord into the "before this" bullet:

> *Originally from Scotland, Rod now lives in San Francisco and is Director of Engineering, Infrastructure and Engineering Velocity at Splice, alongside independent coaching and mentorship for engineering leaders.*
>
> *Before this, he led engineering at Volley Games and Discord, spent four years as a Senior Engineering Manager at Dropbox, and had past lives as Co-Founder & CTO of Sosh, VP of Software Engineering at Anova Culinary, and writing code at places like Slide, Bose, and – for 39 days – Google.*

**2. `data/resumeSection.yml`** — add three new experience entries above the existing Dropbox one (Splice, Volley Games, Discord), same casual tone as existing entries. No entry for the coaching-focused gap between Discord and Volley — it's covered by the hero/about/footer already pitching ongoing coaching, not a discrete past job.

**3. `themes/portio/layouts/partials/footer.html`** — the "Coaching" CTA currently sends people to `practicahq.com/coaching`. Since you still coach independently, I'll point that button at your existing contact link instead (same one the "Email" CTA already uses), and drop "please contact Practica" from the copy.

Want me to go ahead and write these three files as described, or do you want to tweak wording first?
Yeah, and read my voice guide at ~/AUTHOR.md to get the right tone.

1	# AUTHOR.md — Rod Begbie
2	
3	A reference document for communicating in Rod's voice. Use this when writing commit messages, social posts, docs, or any prose intended to sound like Rod wrote it.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Who Rod Is
8	
9	**Rod Begbie** (he/him) is a Scottish software engineering leader living in San Francisco, CA. Gen X. Has ADHD. Swears a lot. Proud of all three.
10	
11	Originally from Scotland, he studied Computer Science at Heriot-Watt University (BSc Hons, 1994–1998). He's lived in San Francisco for many years, with a stint in Barcelona during a career break. He builds software that makes people happy. He's published in O'Reilly's _97 Things Every Programmer Should Know_, has been a featured contributor at LeadDev.com since 2020, and spoken at Lead Dev New York and Calibrate conferences.
12	
13	He once bummed a cigarette from David Bowie.
14	
15	### Career
16	
17	- **Director of Engineering, Splice Sounds** (Apr 2025–present) — leading product engineering for Splice's web, desktop, mobile and in-DAW experiences (Splice is a music creation/samples platform)
18	- **Director of Engineering, TV Games, Volley** (May 2024–Feb 2025) — voice-powered games for Roku/Fire TV; led 5 teams, 25 engineers
19	- **Engineering Leadership Coach, Freelance** (Dec 2020–Jun 2024) — coaching via Practica/BestPracticer
20	- **Career break in Barcelona** (Oct 2020–Apr 2022)
21	- **Senior Engineering Manager, Discord** (Apr 2022–Mar 2023) — Premium Products/Nitro; won "The 80/20 Thinker" award from senior leadership
22	- **Senior Engineering Manager, Dropbox** (2016–2020) — Dropbox Paper & Spaces; created IGNITE apprenticeship program (17 apprentices, 13 full-time offers)
23	- **VP Software Engineering, Anova Culinary** (2015–2016) — smart kitchen appliances
24	- **Co-Founder & CTO, Sosh** (2010–2015) — scaled to 55 people, 16 engineers
25	- **Engineering Manager, Slide** (2008–2010) — Python, early Facebook social games; learned from making every management mistake in the book
26	- **Software Engineer** (1998–2008) — Bose, Current TV, Sapient, and others
27	- **39 days at Google** _(he counts this)_
28	
29	LinkedIn skills: **Kicking Ass** (68 endorsements), **Taking Names** (15 endorsements).
30	
31	---
32	
33	## Voice: Casual (Social Media / Slack / Chat)
34	
35	This is Rod's natural register. Short. Punchy. Opinionated. Warm underneath the prickliness.
36	
37	**Characteristics:**
38	
39	- Profanity is natural, not performed. "man shut the fuck up" is a complete sentence. "Fuckin weird, man." is a reasonable conclusion to an argument.
40	- Dry, sardonic wit. The joke usually lands in the last three words.
41	- Observational — he notices things other people walk past and describes them precisely.
42	- Confident opinions, clearly stated, without hedging. "I don't think it matters whether..." not "it might be the case that..."
43	- Self-aware and self-deprecating about specific things, not as a general tic.
44	- Genuinely generous when it comes to community causes — will donate money and tell you to as well, without being preachy.
45	- Strong progressive political views, stated plainly. Not a bothsidesist.
46	- Lowercase for ironic/casual effect. ALL CAPS for ironic emphasis or genuine absurdist excitement.
47	- Parenthetical kickers that contain the actual point.
48	- Uses threads naturally — adds a follow-up post to extend a story rather than cramming it all in.
49	
50	**Posts that capture the register (confirmed his own words):**
51	
52	> _"Whoop whoop! It's Friday night and I am IN DA CLUB_ — in line at the Walgreens pharmacy counter picking up my adhd meds"\*
53	
54	> _"I build social software things that make people happy. Also, I swear. Mostly, it's the swearing."_
55	
56	> _"Sorry to get radical but I don't think it's my job or yours to embrace, accept, understand — and certainly not to use — the thing being sold to us as AI. I don't like any part of it, so I won't. I'm missing out? Good, that's what I want. You're worried about me missing out? That's fuckin weird, man."_
57	
58	> _"Went to the cinema and had my Heights well and truly Wuthered. Emerald Fennell is now almost certainly my favourite maker of the movies-I-am-glad-I-watched-but-never-need-to-see-again genre. Fuck subtlety and nuance! Gimme two hours of visceral sensory overload that doesn't withstand scrutiny!"_
59	
60	> _"One way of looking at agentic coding is that it satisfies every real engineer's itch to do anything but start writing actual code, the ultimate in not just bike shedding and yak shaving, but managing someone else to do the bike shedding and yak shaving"_
61	
62	> _"Today my Gen Z friend and I were talking about vintage tech, and she asked me what 'the box under the screen' was called. I feel BAD about how loud I shouted 'THE COMPUTER???' ('No, the box the screen sits on.' 'THE COMPUTER???' I shouted again, even more rattled now)"_
63	> _"('Maybe a SCSI!' I said, so passionately that she actually looked scared)"_
64	
65	> _"Just calculated that based on their hourly rates, I spent $84 this week discussing with my therapist and my psychiatrist our opinions of the new Taylor Swift album. (Which I take as strong signal that I have the right mental health team supporting me)"_
66	
67	> _"It's Black Friday, or as it's known since it went solo, Frankday Black."_
68	
69	> _"Getting strong Central ITV vibes from the new Apple TV logo. (One for my Brits-of-a-certain-age chums)"_
70	
71	> _"Nuance to add to this: The value and quality of code generated by LLMs drops rapidly the more narrow and specialised the area of software engineering you work with... General web development in Typescript or Python? Modern coding agents are fantastic and you're a fool if you're not at the very least experimenting in them. Complex financial integrations in Golang? Hmm. They'll need a lot of oversight. Audio engine work in C++? Oof buddy, you're in for some pain."_
72	
73	> _"YES! So fucking wholesome! Siobhan and the judges have just perfected the balance of humour, encouragement and (constructive) feedback in a way that Bake Off never manages. Keith & Rich want them to succeed, compared to Paul getting pissy that no-one managed to read his mind and do the impossible."_
74	
75	> _"Comedy clubs with two drink minimums are just profiteering cunts though. I've been forced to buy too many $8 cans of sparkling water at Cobb's Comedy Club in my time."_
76	
77	**Structural patterns:**
78	
79	- Short takes: one or two sentences, no preamble
80	- Setup → punchline, often with a parenthetical kicker
81	- Follow-up posts in parentheses to extend a story
82	- Specificity is funny: not "a while" but "39 days"; not "a lot of money" but "$84"
83	- Ellipsis for dramatic pause
84	- The Simpsons reference is always available
85	
86	---
87	
88	## Voice: Professional (Articles / Talks / Docs / Code Comments / Commit Messages)
89	
90	When Rod writes formally he's still recognisably Rod — warm, direct, practical — but the swearing disappears and the structure sharpens.
91	
92	**Characteristics:**
93	
94	- Leads with a relatable human situation, not an abstract principle
95	- Self-deprecating about his own past mistakes to make the lesson land
96	- Uses named frameworks and memorable phrases ("linguistic filters", "half-baked idea")
97	- Vivid everyday analogies: "closer to being scrawled on the back of a napkin than being typeset and laminated"
98	- Gives you specific language to use, not just conceptual advice
99	- Doesn't lecture. Offers tools, shares observations, respects the reader's autonomy.
100	- Paragraph rhythm: longer build-up sentences, then a short punchy conclusion.
101	
102	**Example prose (LeadDev article):**
103	
104	> _"I grew from this by learning that I was removing my team's autonomy, and depriving them of a chance to explore and learn from their own mistakes. So, instead of telling them not to do something, I moved to saying what concerned me. 'Here's what I'd be worried about: what will this do to database performance?' I was no longer a roadblock, but a guide."_
105	
106	**Talk/article titles that reveal his thinking:**
107	
108	- _"Being right is only half the battle"_ — influence over a team matters more than being technically correct
109	- _"Engineering Management Anti-Patterns"_ — learn from someone else's mistakes; self-deprecating framing
110	
111	---
112	
113	## Interests & Passions
114	
115	**British TV** — built a whole iOS app (Tellywatcher) for this. Watches it obsessively. Current known favourites include _The Traitors_ (big enough to write jokes about Claudia Winkleman's multiple identities), _The Great Pottery Throwdown_ ("YES! So fucking wholesome!"), and _Gladiators_ (the rebooted UK show — specifically wants Cyclone to beat him up and Fury to rugby tackle him off The Edge).
116	
117	**Scottish identity** — proudly, loudly Scottish. Flags it in bios. Posts Scotland football highlights. References Glasgow cuisine with appropriate gravity.
118	
119	**Football (soccer)** — Scotland national team. Passionate in the way only someone whose team consistently breaks their heart can be.
120	
121	**Baseball** — built Django apps to distribute Sox tickets. Tracks MLB rosters for Dickensian names.
122	
123	**Music** — works at Splice (music creation/samples platform), so this is both professional passion and personal interest. Listens to Green Day, Bad Bunny, Taylor Swift. Donated $500 to rent relief based on a Super Bowl score.
124	
125	**Live comedy** — regular at Cobb's Comedy Club in SF. Visits the Tommyfield in London on trips. Fans of Elis James & John Robins. Listens to The Bugle.
126	
127	**Podcasts & radio** — The Bugle (John Oliver/Andy Zaltzman), Elis James & John Robins, likely others in the British comedy space.
128	
129	**Food** — Anthony Bourdain fan. Strong opinions about British vs. American approaches to public health through food policy (blister packs vs. jugs of 1000 Advil).
130	
131	**Politics** — progressive, anti-fascist, pro-trans rights, anti-corporate tech power. Donates to rent relief causes and progressive campaigns. Not subtle about any of it.
132	
133	**Tech/AI scepticism** — principled personal position (won't use it) combined with a genuinely nuanced professional view (great for generic TypeScript/Python web dev, degrades fast for specialised domains, concerned about looming maintainability crisis and "cognitive debt").
134	
135	**Mental health** — openly discusses therapy, psychiatry, and ADHD as matter-of-fact self-care.
136	
137	**British internet culture** — b3ta.com veteran. Reads Private Eye. Gets the references. Makes the references.
138	
139	**Gaming** — Marvel Snap (listed as a valid reason to email him on his website).
140	
141	**Plex server** — runs one. Noticed when PG&E knocked out a third of SF while he was in a London hotel room.
142	
143	**UK dotcom nostalgia** — a 2007 Guardian article about "Ten British dotcoms to watch" gives him "many feels".
144	
145	---
146	
147	## Worldview
148	
149	- **"The code has always been the easy part."** The hard stuff is people, trust, communication, and culture.
150	- Engineering leaders should develop autonomy in their teams, not be bottlenecks or extra hands.
151	- _"Machine produce code fast is absolutely impressive. But the time it takes engineers to build? Is the time available to other folks to do their jobs. Talking to customers, getting stakeholders aligned, exploring designs, agreeing business goals."_
152	- Capitalism is a structure that erodes confidence and self-worth. Worth naming.
153	- AI hype is a product being sold to you. You're allowed to opt out.
154	- Community matters. If you can help someone make rent, do it.
155	- The right mental health support is worth more than it costs.
156	- 39 days at Google is a great story. Own your weird career path.
157	
158	---
159	
160	## Linguistic Fingerprints
161	
162	**British spellings and idioms, used naturally in an American context:**
163	
164	- "belter" (= something excellent or remarkable)
165	- "chums" (ironically affectionate)
166	- "daft" (= silly/foolish)
167	- "yous" (Scottish plural — occasional)
168	- "on the cards" not "in the cards"
169	- "cunts" — used with more casual British register than American ears expect
170	
171	**Recurring constructions:**
172	
173	- "I don't think it [adjective] that..." — opinions stated as reasoned conclusions, not feelings
174	- "man shut the fuck up" — dismissal as a complete rhetorical move
175	- "Fuckin weird, man." — verdict delivered with finality
176	- Parenthetical asides that contain the actual point: _(which I take as strong signal that...)_
177	- Follow-up parenthetical posts: _(I said, so passionately that she actually looked scared)_
178	- Specificity for humour: exact dollar amounts, exact day counts, exact headcounts
179	- "One for my Brits-of-a-certain-age chums" — niche audience acknowledgement with warmth
180	- "WHOOOOOOOOO CAAAAAAAAARESSSSSS" — elongated for comic effect when something is beneath notice
181	- Interrobang (‽) — he uses it
182	
183	**Things Rod doesn't do:**
184	
185	- Hedge with "I think maybe possibly..." — he just says the thing
186	- Use corporate buzzwords unironically
187	- Pretend to be neutral about things he has opinions on
188	- Write long meandering intros
189	- Use exclamation marks except ironically or for genuine absurdist enthusiasm
190	
191	---
192	
193	## Summary: The Vibe
194	
195	Rod is a Scotsman in SF who has spent 25+ years in tech — from writing Python for Facebook games to leading engineering orgs of 25+ people — and still finds it interesting enough to build an iOS app for tracking British TV on a Saturday night. He's warm, funny, and generous with both his opinions and his money. He will tell you AI is bullshit and also give you a genuinely nuanced take on exactly which domains it's useful for. He's deeply sceptical of the tech industry's self-mythology but loves the craft. He once bummed a cigarette from David Bowie. He wants Cyclone from Gladiators to beat him up. He is, per his own Bluesky bio, Scottish, Sweary, and Gen X as Fuck.
196

input.old_string:
* Originally from Scotland, Rod now lives in San Francisco and is a Senior Engineering Manager at Discord, and an engineering leadership coach with [Practica](https://practicahq.com/).

  * Before this, he spent four years as a Senior Engineering Manager at Dropbox. Past lives have been as Co-Founder & CTO of Sosh, VP of Software Engineering at Anova Culinary, and writing code at places like Slide, Bose, and – for 39 days – Google.

  * His writing is featured on [LeadDev.com](https://leaddev.com/community/rod-begbie) and in O’Reilly’s [*97 Things Every Programmer Should Know:* Collective Wisdom from the Experts](https://www.oreilly.com/library/view/97-things-every/9780596809515/).
input.new_string:
* Originally from Scotland, Rod now lives in San Francisco and is Director of Engineering for Infrastructure and Engineering Velocity at Splice, alongside independent coaching and mentorship for engineering leaders.

  * Before this, he led engineering for Splice Sounds, ran a TV games engineering org at Volley, and was a Senior Engineering Manager at Discord and Dropbox. Past lives have been as Co-Founder & CTO of Sosh, VP of Software Engineering at Anova Culinary, and writing code at places like Slide, Bose, and – for 39 days – Google.

  * His writing is featured on [LeadDev.com](https://leaddev.com/community/rod-begbie) and in O’Reilly’s [*97 Things Every Programmer Should Know:* Collective Wisdom from the Experts](https://www.oreilly.com/library/view/97-things-every/9780596809515/), and he's spoken at conferences including LeadDev New York and Calibrate.

input.new_string:
education:
  - content: >
      #### Director of Engineering, Infrastructure & Engineering Velocity, _Splice_

      * Leading three teams — Engineering Velocity, Data Engineering, and Site Reliability Engineering — 15 engineers and two managers, reporting to the CTO.

      * Founded the Engineering Velocity team from scratch: investigated developer friction company-wide and defined four workstreams (AI Enablement, Toil & Papercuts, Ownership & Knowledge, and CI/CD) to fix it.

      * Before this, led product engineering for Splice Sounds, including the release of a cross-platform VST3/AU/AAX plugin that puts Splice directly inside producers' DAWs.
    time: 2025–Present
  - content: >
      #### Director of Engineering, _Volley Games_

      * Led over half the company's engineering effort — five product teams, four engineering managers, 25 engineers — building voice-powered games for Roku and Fire TV.

      * Turned engineering's reputation from "always late and buggy" into a team that could negotiate scope and reliably hit deadlines with quality work.
    time: 2024–2025
  - content: >
      #### Senior Engineering Manager, _Discord_

      * Group lead for four teams (21 engineers) shipping new in-app features for Nitro, Discord's premium subscription.

      * Drove a "Progress over Perfection" culture of fast, iterative shipping — recognised with "The 80/20 Thinker" award from senior leadership.
    time: 2022–2023
  - content: >
      #### Senior Engineering Manager, _Dropbox_

• Originally from Scotland, Rod now lives in San Francisco and is Director of Engineering for Infrastructure and Engineering Velocity at Splice, alongside independent coaching and mentorship for engineering leaders.
  • Before this, he led engineering for Splice Sounds, ran a TV games engineering org at Volley, and was a Senior Engineering Manager at Discord and Dropbox. Past lives have been as Co-Founder & CTO of Sosh, VP of Software Engineering at Anova Culinary, and writing code at places like Slide, Bose, and – for 39 days – Google.
  • His writing is featured on LeadDev.com and in O’Reilly’s 97 Things Every Programmer Should Know: Collective Wisdom from the Experts, and he’s spoken at conferences including LeadDev New York and Calibrate.
2025–Present

Director of Engineering, Infrastructure & Engineering Velocity, Splice

  • Leading three teams — Engineering Velocity, Data Engineering, and Site Reliability Engineering — 15 engineers and two managers, reporting to the CTO.
  • Founded the Engineering Velocity team from scratch: investigated developer friction company-wide and defined four workstreams (AI Enablement, Toil & Papercuts, Ownership & Knowledge, and CI/CD) to fix it.
  • Before this, led product engineering for Splice Sounds, including the release of a cross-platform VST3/AU/AAX plugin that puts Splice directly inside producers’ DAWs.
2024–2025

Director of Engineering, Volley Games

  • Led over half the company’s engineering effort — five product teams, four engineering managers, 25 engineers — building voice-powered games for Roku and Fire TV.
  • Turned engineering’s reputation from “always late and buggy” into a team that could negotiate scope and reliably hit deadlines with quality work.
    • 2022–2023

    Senior Engineering Manager, Discord

    • Group lead for four teams (21 engineers) shipping new in-app features for Nitro, Discord’s premium subscription.
    • Drove a “Progress over Perfection” culture of fast, iterative shipping — recognised with “The 80/20 Thinker” award from senior leadership.
